displayTitle: 2026-02-19 - Environment Committee - Ministry for the Environment and Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet, Long-term Insights Briefing 2025
topic: Ministry for the Environment and Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet, Long-term Insights Briefing 2025
description: Thursday, 19th February 2026 | Environment Committee Ministry for the Environment and Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet, Long-term Insights Briefing 2025 Hearing of evidence: Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet Ben King, Chief Executive Sean Bolton, Deputy Chief Executive Peter Ranger, Senior Advisor Ministry for the Environment James Palmer, Chief Executive Dr Alison Collins, Chief Science Advisor Katherine Wilson, General Manager – Adaptation Dr Alice Cleland, Manager – Insights Paloma Hartevelt, Acting Chief Advisor
descriptionSummary: Ministry for the Environment and Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet, Long-term Insights Briefing 2025. Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet. Ben King, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Morning. Morning. Welcome, welcome. Good to see you all. And we will, we're obviously, we're faring our way through the briefing. It's about is our third go, I think. It isn't. Um, look, uh, welcome. So, um, if you quickly introduce yourselves and we'll just carry on from we left off team. Great. Thanks. Excellent. Thanks, Mr. Chair.

Um, and committee members, this is the, this Is the, Hmm. Long-term Insights. Long-term Insights Briefing. Yeah. Yeah. So our first, First one, first field. Okay. So, um, thanks Chair and committee members for the opportunity to speak to you today on our long term Insights Briefing, building New Zealand's long-term Resilience to Hazards. My name is Ben King. I'm the Secretary and Chief Executive of the Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet.

I'm here with my colleague, James Palmer, who is, uh, the, the Secretary and Chief Executive for Environment and the Ministry for Environment. Um, while this is my first time, uh, before this committee, I'd like to acknowledge the bipartisan approach, uh, that is taken in general on emergency management and national resilience.

The committee I usually report to the, uh, government Administration Committee, um, the chair, uh, Camilla Blich and Mr. Mitchell have a really good work relationship building allege, uh, their approach. And I was delighted that James tells me, um, it's a similar approach here. I'd like to start by acknowledging, uh, those who tragically lost their lives in January's, uh, weather events, our thoughts, uh, with those communities affected.

And I'd also like to acknowledge the impact weather events more recently, uh, across the Lower North Island, while how long-term Insights Briefing was prepared before these events. Uh, they do bring into focus the stark reality of the challenges we face as an agent, in line with the intent and guidance for long-term insights briefings.

Our briefing, uh, was produced independently of ministers and does not comment on government policy or provide recommendations. So it's a think piece, a piece of assessment intended to look beyond the immediate political horizon and help us all parliament, the public and the private sector plan for future challenges. The focus of our briefing is national resilience.

We define this as the ability to prevent, minimize, absorb, adapt to recover from, and transform through shocks and stresses resilience and enhance safety, security, prosperity of our people. Historically, new Zealand's approach to risk management has been largely reactive.

However, we know that human and financial costs of that model, uh, unacceptably high. So our briefing argues for a strategic, proactive approach, making the investments and the hard choices now that will prevent loss and harm in the future. To be proactive, we must be clear-eyed about the landscape.

We are in New Zealand as a multi-aged environment, and we are consistently ranked amongst the countries with the highest disaster risk in the world. National Risk Register identifies 14 major hazards, but in this briefing we have mostly focused on the big six, those hazards that either have the potential for catastrophic impact or occur so frequently that the accumulative cost is immense.

DPMC takes the challenges outlined in this briefing. Seriously, over the last year, we have reprioritized our resourcing, allowing us to increase the number of staff we have dedicated to implementing the National Risk and Resilience Framework. From three to 13, these staff are focused on working across government to proactively build resilience.

And late last year, we've supported the Prime Minister to deliver the first ever national Risk and Resilience report back to cabinet, which identified opportunities to strengthen accountability and oversight. We also reshaped the governance system, which, uh, which oversees the, uh, national Resilience System, uh, with the National Security Board and Emergency Management Board, and a National Hazard Risk Reduction Board reporting into a National Resilience Board as the umbrella.

Well done. Thanks very much for the opportunity to appear before you today. Um, we look forward to the discussion of the, of the Briefing. And pass now to James. Thank you being Koto, uh, chair committee members. Um, thanks Ben for the introduction and also acknowledging the tragic loss of life, uh, and the ongoing disruption caused by our most recent severe weather.

Uh, just also wanna acknowledge, uh, the D-P-M-C-N-M-E fee team who developed this briefing, uh, a number of whom are with us, uh, today, and just c in the insights that they've provided, and also the way in which the agencies, uh, have worked together, uh, for the ministry. Working with DPMC, uh, on this has been a particular, uh, privilege.

Uh, shortly after our team started this work, uh, the public sector was mobilized, uh, once again alongside communities to deal, uh, the latest real life demonstration of the very issues that raised, and that was, uh, severe weather, uh, in the, uh, Nelson Tasman, uh, district. And throughout the time that we've developed this, uh, and there have been ongoing events, I've been struck by two key thoughts.

The first of which is that as a country, we need to stop being surprised. Mm-hmm. Uh, events like these are not anomalies. Um, they're known features of life in New Zealand. Uh, they've had them many times in the past, and they'll continue, uh, to have a science has told us for decades that as a small island nation, nations straddling a tectonic plate boundary and surrounded by warming oceans.

Our hazard profile is not a matter of if, but it is when second, there is an enormous opportunity in that predictability. If we accept the certainty of these events, we can become one of the most prepared nations in the world. New Zealand currently loses 0.6% of GDP annually on average to natural hazards.

That's nearly twice the OECD average. Since 2010, we've spent $64 billion as a country on hazard related costs, and 97% of that expenditure has been on response and recovery, and only 3% has been spent on reducing risk in the first place. That balance, uh, is unsustainable and that core concept we wanted to highlight, uh, with this briefing, how do we shift as a country from being reactive and focusing on recovery to expecting, anticipating, preparing for, and reducing, uh, risk ahead of harm?

Han our briefing outlines, uh, what Ben referred to as six. Uh, the big six hazards, the major, uh, risks that shape New Zealand's resilience landscape. We begin with earthquakes still the most consequential of our hazards. Uh, the Alpine fault, the southern margin, uh, and the Wellington Fault all hold the potential to, to, to, to rupture in ways that could radically transform our cities and infrastructure and our daily lives with national consequences.

Uh, I particularly turn, um, the, uh, the, the committee's, uh, attention toward the, uh, the Alpine fault. And the briefing is noted, the 75% probability of a rupture within the next 50 years. Uh, the Alpine fault is unusual, uh, in international terms for its predictability.

Uh, it does rupture consistently, reliably, every 300 years, and it is now overdue, and there is an 80% probability of that being a more than a zero, uh, a an eight magnitude, uh, earthquake with massive consequences for particularly the south island, but also New Zealand's infrastructure, tourism, uh, and economy alongside the sits volcanic activity, uh, a continual presence in our geology, uh, and an unexpected eruption in tamaki makoto, or renewed unrest in Taranaki, uh, would bring swift far reaching consequences for communities

and the economy, uh, and also draw the committee's attention to the probabilities and the briefing around, uh, a interruption, not taranaki. Uh, it's something we should anticipate this century. We also face, face the threat of tsunami, less frequent, but devastating when they come, often in the wake of some large earthquakes.

Uh, we already fear and capable of compounding the damage at extraordinary speed. Uh, and the geological re record shows that New Zealand has been hit by large tsunamis along its coastline many, many times. So again, not a matter of if, just a matter of when severe weather has become the hazard we see and feel.

Most often the storms, landslides, and floods of recent months are not outliers. Our warnings, these events are clustering, intensifying, and interacting with where and how we've built our homes, roads, and infrastructure. Some hazards feel newer, even if the risks are very real. Space weather, for example, where a major solar storm could disrupt the satellite based systems that underpin everything from our trading networks to our emergency services.

And pandemics, of course, remain an enduring risk, reminding us that biological threats can escalate quickly and globally across all six. One. Truth is constant. A disaster only occurs when a hazard meets people assets and the systems that are exposed and vulnerable. We cannot stop the earth from moving or the weather from intensifying at least, uh, in the short to medium term.

But we can shape what is placed in harm's way and how well it can withstand the shocks to come with. That requires systems, legal planning, financial, digital, scientific that consistently drives safer choices. And a cornerstone of that system is reliable, accessible, decision ready, hazard and risk information.

This information is in every sense, national infrastructure. Yet in many of the data sets that should, uh, anchor our decisions, such as landside, slide susceptibility, stormwater capacity, rainfall, intensities, soil stability, flood extents, they're all fragmented, aging, and in, uh, inconsistently maintained and funded at present, recent events have shown the cost of that gap.

When information is unclear or unavailable, decisions default to reaction and reaction is slower, more expensive, and more damaging for communities. But information is only one part of the resilience landscape. A risk profile is shaped by broader forces. Some that increase our exposure and some that strengthen our ability to withstand shocks On the exposure side are our development patterns.

Most of our towns and cities built on river mouths and coastal mouths, our geographic isolation, our concentrated economy, and an aging population, and a growing dependence on digital systems, all occurring against the backdrop of global instability, stable institutions, deep scientific capability, high levels of social trust, and strong partnerships with across central government, local government, iwi, industry, and community can be a strength, uh, when they are nurtured.

Crucially, hazards do not operate in isolation. Risks are increasingly cascading. A flood triggers a power failure, which disables communication, which then escalates into a health emergency. Our systems must be designed with this interconnectedness in mind. This is why our briefing emphasizes the unavoidable choices ahead.

None are cost free. Delaying decisions only increases the eventual price. And these choices are challenging, not because the risks are unknown, but 'cause information is imperfect. The incentives of different groups vary, and the trade-offs are real, being explicit about those trade-offs, which is gained.

Uh, what is gained, what has given up and why allows decisions to be made in transparent, durable, and fair manner. The encouraging news is that momentum is building. Ben's already referred to the investment made in Department of Prime Minister and Cabinet around the National Risk and Resilience framework. The National Adaptation plan, and the work that we've done with this committee in the last few years around adaptation and further policy and legislative, uh, changes to come before this committee later this year.

And reforms to our planning and environmental management system, which was of course before, uh, this committee right now, as well as reform to the science system and to, uh, the emergency management system, are all starting to build a much more coherent national approach. New digital tools will for the first time, provide a single national channel for consistent and assured hazard information to flow into those statutory plans that you are overseeing the policy for the spatial plans and the regulatory plans, infrastructure investment, uh,

which is topical, obviously with the National Infrastructure Plan, uh, released this week. And local adaptation decisions that will be part of the, uh, adaptation legislation before committee later this year are replacing a current system where 78 councils produce 78 different sets of hazard maps, models and risk layers.

All that differ, all with their gaps, uh, in coverage. So we're standing at a rare moment where the resource management reforms, the climate adaptation, the emergency management reforms, digital enablement, and the science system reforms are all actually converging together. They give us the chance to design a risk and planning system that's fit for the future, rather than patching together one from the past.

And there is scope to go even further by empowering our communities, harnessing data and technologies like AI, designing smarter and more adaptive infrastructure, including the use of nature-based solutions and natural infrastructure, and strengthening our investment models that spread risk and accelerate resilience. Ultimately, national resilience is not about just about reducing losses, it's actually about unlocking gains, a safer, more resilient, and more insurable.

Uh, New Zealand is one where people and businesses thrive, not only when conditions are easy, but also when they are hard. Resilience is a long game, and it requires all of us central government, local government, iwi, industry, and households all to play their part. The future does not simply happen to us. It's something that we can shape together.

Thank you for your time, and we welcome your questions. Thank you very much. You both, it's a very sobering way start. Okay. Uh, look, I've got a range of questions we'll, uh, start with Thank you. Um, thank you both for your work on this and your teams at the two agencies. Um, and I think it speaks to the importance of having think pieces like this that sits outside of election cycles and independent of ministers.

So I will just say that it's a shame that all of these are gonna be asked except for one, um, going forward. Um, but just a couple of questions that I, that I'll start with, if that's all right. Mr. Chu, um, you have laid out quite clearly, um, throughout the report, sort of, you've alluded to existing pieces of work that are building momentum.

You've made the point that we are not sort of starting from scratch. There is work to, uh, towards this. I wondered whether you also, I took a look at pieces of work, um, that perhaps may not be continuing now that would've contributed to towards the goals that you've mentioned. Um, you've mentioned the need for social license, so things like the social cohesion work program, things like that, that would help build, um, uh, resilience and social license.

Did you look at other things that perhaps might add to this work program? I'm, I'm happy to, um, have a, have a have a go this. So the, I mean, social cohesion is such an important, um, underpinning piece of fabric, which goes through basically any risk or any threat or any hazard. Um, social occasions becomes, um, really important.

So at a, at a broader level across governments, so not necessarily in the context of this, but, um, without, we are long and actively talking about how we continue to build the programs, how we, um, get at, uh, uh, you know, helping people who are in need, anticipating where needs will come, building the, the social cohesion, doing it through, um, a rank, you know, through SD through ethnic communities, and also working with the communities themselves.

So we are, it's a active discussion and something we are pushing in on. Uh, so Has stopped As No, no, no. Well, our, we as a, as a system, as a public service, we certainly haven't stopped, um, the work and we haven't stopped the, um, the understanding and the commitment to knowing that social cohesion is something that we need to continue to build.

So part of that then is trust in institutions. You've laid this out in your report as well. Um, there are other think pieces that point to the fact that globally trust in institutions is declining. Um, and I wondered whether through this piece of work, you had perhaps seen some good examples overseas, the international models that you've laid out in this report, uh, specifically points to response to security, um, and defense sort of measures perhaps based on risk and resilience in those countries.

But outside of that, and if you look at what we need, what pillars we need to build trust in institutions, was there anything that you came across that isn't in this report that you thought might be useful? Uh, well, I don't, I don't think so. Let me just check. Yeah, I, I might, I might just make one, one comment.

I'm aware that the, um, the global Edelman survey that's done of, uh, of trust around institutions globally, which is considered to be rigorous and, uh, internationally, uh, consistent from a benchmarking point of view, is certainly showing that, uh, trust and confidence in our science institutions is holding up.

Mm-hmm. And I think that's something for us to be, uh, very grateful for. And I think part of, uh, the, the challenge for the science system is it's communication with communities and, and meeting communities where they're at. And it is something that I know the government through the science reforms is particularly, uh, focused as is on the translation of science to, uh, real world outcomes.

And there is a particular investment focus around hazards and resilience. So I think, um, we've got, uh, we, we've made some, some progress in that regard, but I think part of what we've tried to highlight here is that there is more work to do to build public awareness around the hazard scape. And science will be critical to that.

And trust and confidence in our science institutions, I think, can help depoliticize to an extent, because I think communities do consider, uh, scientists to generally be apolitical, uh, and be, um, uh, neutral on these questions. Mm-hmm. Many of the issues, particularly in relation to climate change, as the committee knows, uh, have become politically contested.

Uh, and as a consequence, I think, uh, letting the science speak for itself, uh, as a way of ensuring that we build trust and confidence, uh, in, in a way that's harder, I guess, for democratic institutions to do. So it's in support of local government and central government in the decisions that will need to be taken. Yeah. Thanks, Julian. You supple on up on that, up On that, if I just, just in terms of, uh, impacts of projects that aren't funded that would potentially help New Zealand be better prepared for these kind of

resilience outcomes that we're trying to achieve here. What about the very absence of the Climate Resilience fund? Sorry, the Climate Emergency Resilience Response Fund. Um, is that in itself, the absence of that, you know, contributing to our lack of resilience in this area?

So there has continued to be investment in, uh, uh, flood management. Mm-hmm. Uh, as the committee will be aware, and the current investment in infrastructure more broadly, um, not just roading, uh, uh, in a, in a range of areas is, uh, helping to build resilience. So I, I, I think it would be, um, incorrect to characterize the, uh, the investment in national infrastructure resilience as, uh, having been turned off or no longer continuing despite the discontinuation of that fund.

So there is still, uh, uh, there is still, um, a significant investment going in there. I think what the national infrastructure plan highlighted earlier this week, however, is that this is a very long game. Mm-hmm. And there is a lot of investment required over a very long period of time. That's right. And I think it's, um, it's a statement of fact that New Zealand has a major infrastructure deficit and that we have a lot of catch up to do over, over a long period of time.

The affordability of that and the rate at which we can build further resilience and, and, uh, maintain our assets and build new ones are all choices for New Zealanders, obviously, in terms of a, you know, willingness to pay and priorities for, for investment. But yeah. So David, yeah, it is supplementary and, and thank you for the report.

I think this is, uh, word is enlightening and particularly with certain risks that you sort of look step back and you think, oh, yeah, I suppose that is absolutely right. But extending on what LAN's saying then in your commentary, just then, James, um, at the end of the day, this committee is, has a, has a very specific purpose, a very, uh, specific job.

And I think what would help us is probably having greater visibility of the spend of, with regards to proactive as well as reactive. You've spoken to the many billions of dollars that are spent over the years, but for us to actually understand better, uh, what is actually happening, where the trends with regards to it, I think would be very helpful.

And, uh, an example that comes to mind is, uh, the, uh, uh, commissioner for the environment with what he does with the environmental spend, with his, uh, zaki, um, graph and all that sort of stuff. I think that would be very helpful for our committee to be able to work out what are the questions and who to talk to to see whether we're doing the best we can possibly do.

Secondly, as a supplementary is about that, uh, priorities. As you say, James, there's lots of spend that's on the horizon, but how do we go about prior we prioritizing what we're spending on, uh, recently we've put a big effort into flood management, uh, and that what's next, cab off the rank, all that sort of stuff.

I think those are the questions that I think this committee should be prepared to be asking whatever entity that we've got in front of you. So it's, it's actually more of a comment that I think we could improve upon the information that would help us to do our job. Okay. Okay. That's Good. Good. Thank you. Chair. Can I suggest that we come back to the committee, uh, in, in writing with, uh, some response to either the availability of the information or with the information?

That'd be great. Thank you. Appreciate that. Right. So Julianne in the primary. Yeah. Um, well, actually it's pretty related to the discussion we've been having. Thank you so much for the briefing. Um, given the need to have it be long term and outside political cycles, um, like to what extent should we be thinking about something like a parliamentary commissioner for catastrophic risk that's sitting a little bit independent of the government of the day that can advise parliament and do some of that long-term thinking

and, you know, offer off insights to government departments because the government of the day has its own priorities it's selected on, which are often shorter term. Yeah. Maybe I'll, I'll add a, um, first response on that. We've actually got a, a range of tools in the transparency around, um, ing and decisions that are made against those becoming more, um, you know, there's much more information going into the public domain.

So, um, three years ago there was no national risk and resilience sort of framework, and now we've got it up. It's, it's, uh, cabinet document, it's, it's public, and the risks are against them. And at the moment, we are going through and actually just doing a, um, line by line or a risk by risk review of, um, what's your maximum credible event, what's your response readiness, and what's the, the golf between them?

Because of course, you can never, um, or you could throw a whole lot of resource at one particular risk and never actually yeah, be completely ready. So I actually think, um, your, your best tool is actually, um, the transparency of the information and, and the building awareness with the public because, um, that information's available to, to all of you, but actually more importantly, is available to communities.

Communities need to understand, um, or they'd benefit from understanding where there are gaps, thinking about how they, as a society, as an individual, as a family, as a, as a community, or as a, as an iwi group, um, actually get themselves ready for particular risk and, and how they're shaped. So that's a long way of saying, um, I'm not sure that you would need a independent commissioner.

Um, if there was a sense that there wasn't enough information or that choices weren't available and able to prosecute them and ask questions about them and challenge them, then maybe there'd be a, a role for a independent commissioner. Um, the report identifies that local government iwi community responses and readiness is really important.

Um, local government revenue at the moment is only about 4% of GDP, where central government's 33%. Um, like, is local government really sufficiently funded to provide and do that community building and information sharing that and infrastructure investment that might be needed at a local level?

Or does, you know, parliament need to think about how we can better resource local government to, to do this work? Yeah, I, I, it's probably outside of the scope of our, um, of our, of our briefing, um, to be honest, because, um, we, we've identified the, the players and the actors, but how, um, various entities of funding are, I mean, I really welcome In the parliament, The acknowledgement of nature-based solutions and the, you know, huge potential there.

What we've found is, um, with the shift to the new water entities, um, in Wellington, for example, there's local, locally elected representatives who really want to be able to, um, invest in these nature-based solutions. The community wants it, but now the decisions are gonna be made by these separate water entities who are mainly thinking about reticulated solutions.

And, and council has, again, doesn't necessarily have the capital to be able to make those investments. The water entities might benefit from it, but they're just thinking about pipes and their responsibility. They don't, they're not thinking about the water before it gets into the pipes. How, how can we as parliamentarians, enable local government to be able to make, or who, who who really has the incentive to think about the nature-based solutions?

So I'll make a comment on that. So the, one of the reasons that, that we wanted to highlight that, uh, uh, is that, uh, nature-based solutions often, uh, cost effective over the longer term. Uh, and putting a pipe in the ground can often be, uh, a good short term solution, but longer term present issues including ongoing ma maintenance and renewal.

So, um, I think lifting up the, the, the broader sentiment that the, uh, that the briefings trying to, uh, provoke is around longer term thinking and our investment horizons around preparedness, including an infrastructure investment. And that comes down to a question of priorities for communities.

And I think for, for local government in particular, uh, uh, but also for central government, um, you know, we're acutely aware of the affordability challenges for, uh, many communities and households right now with, with rating burdens as well as insurance on top of that, as well as, um, the broader economic backdrop.
